concepts.name: Classical Idea of Oxidation
concepts.explanation: Oxidation originally meant addition of oxygen to a substance or removal of hydrogen from it. It was later extended to include addition of electronegative elements or removal of electropositive elements.
concepts.formula_reaction: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO (addition of oxygen); 2H₂S + O₂ → 2S + 2H₂O (removal of hydrogen)

concepts.name: Classical Idea of Reduction
concepts.explanation: Reduction originally meant removal of oxygen from a substance. It was later extended to include removal of electronegative elements, addition of hydrogen, or addition of electropositive elements.
concepts.formula_reaction: 2HgO → 2Hg + O₂ (removal of oxygen); 2FeCl₃ + H₂ → 2FeCl₂ + 2HCl (removal of chlorine)

concepts.name: Redox Reactions in Terms of Electron Transfer
concepts.explanation: Oxidation is defined as loss of electron(s), and reduction is defined as gain of electron(s). A redox reaction consists of two half-reactions: one oxidation and one reduction.

concepts.name: Oxidising and Reducing Agents
concepts.explanation: An oxidising agent (oxidant) accepts electrons and gets reduced. A reducing agent (reductant) donates electrons and gets oxidised. In 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl, Cl₂ is the oxidant and Na is the reductant.

concepts.name: Competitive Electron Transfer Reactions
concepts.explanation: Metals compete for electrons in redox reactions. A more reactive metal can displace a less reactive metal from its salt solution. The reducing tendency of metals follows the order: Zn > Cu > Ag.
concepts.formula_reaction: Zn(s) + Cu²⁺(aq) → Zn²⁺(aq) + Cu(s); Cu(s) + 2Ag⁺(aq) → Cu²⁺(aq) + 2Ag(s)

concepts.name: Oxidation Number – Definition and Significance
concepts.explanation: Oxidation number is the charge an atom would have if all shared electron pairs were assigned to the more electronegative atom. It is a bookkeeping tool to track electron shifts in redox reactions.

concepts.name: Rules for Assigning Oxidation Numbers
concepts.explanation: Rules include: free elements have ON 0; monoatomic ions have ON equal to charge; oxygen is -2 (except peroxides -1, superoxides -½, OF₂ +2); hydrogen is +1 (except metal hydrides -1); sum of ONs in a compound is zero; in a polyatomic ion, sum equals ion charge.

concepts.name: Stock Notation
concepts.explanation: Stock notation expresses the oxidation state of a metal as a Roman numeral in parentheses after the metal's symbol. Examples: Fe(II)O (ferrous oxide), Fe(III)₂O₃ (ferric oxide), Sn(IV)Cl₄ (stannic chloride).

concepts.name: Oxidation Number vs Oxidation State – Redox Definitions
concepts.explanation: Oxidation is an increase in oxidation number; reduction is a decrease in oxidation number. A redox reaction involves a change in oxidation numbers of the interacting species.

concepts.name: Combination Redox Reactions
concepts.explanation: A combination reaction where two or more reactants form a single product, and at least one reactant is in elemental form, is a redox reaction. Examples: C + O₂ → CO₂; 3Mg + N₂ → Mg₃N₂.

concepts.name: Decomposition Redox Reactions
concepts.explanation: A decomposition reaction where a compound breaks down into simpler substances, with at least one product in elemental form, is a redox reaction. Examples: 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂; 2KClO₃ → 2KCl + 3O₂.

concepts.name: Displacement Redox Reactions – Metal Displacement
concepts.explanation: A metal in a compound is displaced by another more reactive metal. Example: Zn + CuSO₄ → Cu + ZnSO₄. These are used in metallurgy (e.g., thermite reaction: Cr₂O₃ + 2Al → Al₂O₃ + 2Cr).

concepts.name: Displacement Redox Reactions – Non-metal Displacement
concepts.explanation: Non-metals like hydrogen or halogens can be displaced. Active metals displace H₂ from water or acids (e.g., Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂). Halogens displace less reactive halogens from their salts (e.g., Cl₂ + 2Br⁻ → 2Cl⁻ + Br₂).

concepts.name: Disproportionation Reactions
concepts.explanation: In disproportionation, the same element is simultaneously oxidised and reduced. The element must have at least three oxidation states, and the reactant is in the intermediate state. Examples: 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂; Cl₂ + 2OH⁻ → ClO⁻ + Cl⁻ + H₂O.

concepts.name: Balancing Redox Reactions – Oxidation Number Method
concepts.explanation: Steps: (1) Write skeletal equation; (2) Assign oxidation numbers; (3) Calculate increase/decrease and equalise by multiplying; (4) Add H⁺ or OH⁻ to balance charge; (5) Add H₂O to balance O and H atoms. Used for ionic reactions in acidic/basic media.

concepts.name: Balancing Redox Reactions – Half-Reaction Method
concepts.explanation: Steps: (1) Write ionic equation; (2) Separate into oxidation and reduction half-reactions; (3) Balance atoms (other than O and H); (4) Balance O with H₂O and H with H⁺; (5) Balance charge with electrons; (6) Equalise electrons and add half-reactions. For basic medium, add OH⁻ to neutralise H⁺.

concepts.name: Redox Titrations
concepts.explanation: Redox titrations determine the strength of an oxidant or reductant. Permanganate (MnO₄⁻) acts as a self-indicator (pink colour). Other indicators like diphenylamine or starch-iodine (with thiosulphate) are used for redox end-point detection.

concepts.name: Fractional Oxidation Numbers
concepts.explanation: Fractional oxidation numbers (e.g., C in C₃O₂ is 4/3, S in Na₂S₄O₆ is 2.5) are averages. The actual structure shows atoms of the same element in different whole-number oxidation states. Example: in S₄O₆²⁻, two S are +5 and two S are 0.

concepts.name: Redox Couples and Electrode Potentials
concepts.explanation: A redox couple consists of the oxidised and reduced forms of a substance (e.g., Zn²⁺/Zn). The electrode potential measures the tendency of a species to gain or lose electrons. Standard electrode potential (E°) is measured against the standard hydrogen electrode (E° = 0.00 V).

concepts.name: Standard Electrode Potential (E°) and Reactivity
concepts.explanation: A negative E° means the redox couple is a stronger reducing agent than H⁺/H₂. A positive E° means it is a weaker reducing agent. The more negative the E°, the stronger the reducing agent. Examples: Li⁺/Li (-3.05 V) is very strong reducing agent; F₂/F⁻ (+2.87 V) is very strong oxidising agent.

concepts.name: Daniell Cell
concepts.explanation: The Daniell cell consists of Zn/Zn²⁺ and Cu²⁺/Cu half-cells connected by a salt bridge. Electrons flow from Zn (anode, oxidation) to Cu (cathode, reduction) through the external circuit. The cell reaction is Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu.
